extra.reasoning:
The monotonicity property is VIOLATED. Enum order is self_care < routine < urgent < emergent.

Rule r2 (IF confusion THEN acuity := self_care) has priority 100, far above r1 and r3 (priority 2), yet it sets acuity to the LOWEST value, self_care. So whenever confusion is true, r2 dominates and forces acuity to self_care regardless of the other inputs.

Counterexample (compare same inputs with confusion toggled):
- Baseline: confusion=false, hemoptysis=true, slurred_speech=false. Only r1 matches (priority 2), so acuity := urgent.
- After setting confusion=true (others unchanged): hemoptysis=true, slurred_speech=false, confusion=true. Now r1 (priority 2 -> urgent) and r2 (priority 100 -> self_care) both match; r2 wins on higher priority, so acuity := self_care.

Setting confusion=true changed acuity from urgent to self_care, which is LOWER in the enum order. This contradicts the property that setting confusion true never lowers acuity. The witness_assignment gives the full input assignment for the violating (confusion=true) state.
